text: The intraoperative view shows the abdomen of a male patient with rectal cancer undergoing laparoscopic anterior resection. The imperial mesenteric vein is ligated, and the mesentery is prepared. Under white light, a transection point is marked by an arrow. The screen displays three small boxes on the left and a larger box: the top left shows a white light image, the middle left displays a near-infrared signal in black and white, and the bottom left presents a hybrid image with ICG fluorescence appearing green.

Indocyanine green (ICG) is injected intravenously, causing the surrounding tissue to fluoresce green under near-infrared imaging. The planned transection point fails to show inflow, while more proximal sections of the colon begin to fluoresce, indicating better perfusion. Over six minutes, the green fluorescence migrates distally, complicating the identification of an optimal transection point due to the shifting transition zone.
